summary: DHH reflects on his evolution from an aggressive tech evangelist to a more accepting stance on programming language diversity. He recounts how Rails' success came not from pointed arguments against competitors, but from the famous 15-minute demo video that simply showed what was possible. He argues that you can't convert people through dunking or debate — you can only attract those already dissatisfied with their current tools. He concludes that programmers come in different intellectual shapes, and finding the language that clicks with your mind is more important than any objective trade-off analysis.
key_points: Rails succeeded because of a demo showing what was possible, not through arguments against competing frameworks
You can't dunk someone into submission — attraction works better than persuasion in technology adoption
Programmers have different intellectual shapes and sizes, and ecosystem diversity is a feature not a bug
Finding a language that connects you to an enduring source of motivation matters more than objective trade-off analysis
Technical discussions are more effective when framed as 'here's what I like and why' rather than crusading against alternatives
money_quotes: You simply can't dunk someone into submission, and it's usually counterproductive if you try.
I've never seen someone who was head'over'heels in love with, say, functional programming be won over by arguments for objected-oriented programming.
The fact that working for the web allows for such diverse ecosystem choices is an incredible feature, not a bug.
Half the programming conundrum lies in connecting to an enduring source of motivation.
There is no universal set of trade-offs that'll make something objectively 'work best'.
I would give up all the fancy cars I have in a heartbeat, if I was made to choose between them and programming.
Programming is one of those 'best things' that is virtually free to everyone in the Western world.
key_insight: Technology adoption is driven not by winning arguments against alternatives, but by demonstrating something compelling and letting dissatisfied programmers discover it for themselves.
